message.content: Adversaries may exploit resource forks to conceal malicious code or executables, thereby evading detection and bypassing security measures. Resource forks, though deprecated, allow structured storage of various resources like images, icons, and code. They can be identified using commands like `ls -l@` or `xattr -l`. Despite their deprecation, adversaries can still use them to hide malicious data, which can be executed from an attached resource fork at a specified offset. This technique can obfuscate or encrypt content until execution, making detection challenging. Notable malware such as Keydnap and OSX/Shlayer have utilized resource forks to disguise their presence and evade traditional security scanners. Mitigation strategies include monitoring for unusual extended attributes, employing comprehensive endpoint detection and response (EDR) solutions, and educating users about the risks associated with deprecated macOS features.
